name: Pouet Escollerat de l'Ase
equipment: A rope of approximately 10 m, to be tied to a pine tree next to the pit entrance
description: A small but interesting pit near Cova del Tuputut, discovered on February 9, 2008. The circular entrance is 0.60 m wide and leads to a well-decorated alveolar cavity featuring stalagmites, stalactites, small flags, and fine soda straws. Water marks on the walls indicate historical water levels. The skeleton of a donkey and some goat remains are found inside.
full_description:
Near the Cova del Tuputut lies this small and interesting pit which, although its dimensions are not very large in either depth or width, is nonetheless interesting as it is a most charming pit that I found on February 9, 2008, a few weeks before its exploration.

The dimensions of the circular-shaped entrance are 0.60 m, and I have covered it with branches to prevent anyone from accidentally falling inside. Once the branches covering the entrance are removed, we only need to tie the rope to the pine tree next to it to begin the short descent which, after a small overhang, will place us on top of a detritus cone from which we can observe that we are in a highly decorated alveolar-type cavity where stalagmites, stalactites, small flags, and very fine soda straws can be seen.

It is worth noting in this small cavity the water marks on the wall that indicate the different levels the water reached during some of its stages, with the highest one approximately one meter and twenty centimeters from the ground, as some of the formations hanging from the ceiling clearly indicate.

Very close to the pit entrance, a significant collapse can be seen, apparently not very remote in time, which starts practically from the surface, so everything seems to indicate that very soon perhaps a new entrance to the pit may open up.

A sort of crawlway about one meter high seems to indicate the continuation of the pit, but it only seems that way as it is not so. However, I recommend going into it as far as it allows and then looking upward to contemplate a beautifully decorated corner.

The skeleton of an equine, possibly a donkey, is found in a corner of the cavity alongside that of some goats. The first one certainly could not have fallen into the pit due to the dimensions of the entrance, so everything indicates that someone took it upon themselves, once the animal was butchered, to throw its remains inside — a very common custom widespread on the island.

It is advisable to bring a camera when visiting this cavity, as we will not regret it.

We can complete the visit to this pit by visiting either the Cova del Tuputut, which is a little more than one hundred and fifty meters away at the antenna road junction, or the impressive 'Avenc de s'Esquerda,' which was also found and explored for the first time by Mallorca Verde.
